chain_of_thought: Top native frame is Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum inside libUbikeyJni.so, below that are certificate/ASN.1 conversion routines; this is application crypto parsing code, not an allocator/sanitizer frame.

Tools are unavailable (404 from Ghidra MCP), so I cannot decompile, but function naming is descriptive: it likely walks ASN.1 structures to compute child element counts or offsets while converting an ASN.1-encoded certificate into X509 structures.

The JNI bridge jGetCertInfo takes a byte[] and returns byte[]. The fuzzer harness fuzz_one_input is providing arbitrary certificate blob bytes directly to this JNI, so the ASN.1 parsing stack is clearly operating on attacker-controlled input with minimal or no pre-validation on Java side (method signature suggests raw bytes passed in).

Typical bugs in ASN.1 child-number / length computation are out-of-bounds reads/writes, integer overflows, or invalid memory accesses when malformed length/offset fields are not checked; these are well-known classes of vulnerabilities in certificate parsing stacks and are often exploitable (e.g., OpenSSL/ASN.1 bugs).

Because the process termination is explicitly reported for Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum, there is a crash in this parsing code when handling malformed ASN.1; given that ASN.1 values and lengths are derived from untrusted certificate bytes passed in from Java, this is a realistic vulnerability path and not a harness-only artefact.

We lack exact code evidence (cannot see whether it is an OOB read, write, or assertion abort), so cannot assert critical severity, but given the context (native parsing of untrusted ASN.1 with a crash), we should classify as a likely memory safety / parsing vulnerability with at least DoS impact and potential for more.

Thus, this should be treated as a genuine vulnerability with medium-to-high risk, but confidence is somewhat reduced because we cannot inspect the implementation; we therefore pick medium severity, high-ish confidence that it is a real app bug reachable by malformed cert input.
reasons: Crash occurs in native ASN.1 parsing routine Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum, not in harness or allocator
JNI bridge Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetCertInfo processes attacker-controlled certificate bytes from Java without visible length/structure validation
Malformed ASN.1 commonly triggers out-of-bounds or invalid memory access in child-count/length computation routines, causing process termination
Crash path is realistic in production whenever app parses untrusted or semi-trusted certificates (e.g., smart card, remote server)
recommendations: Review and harden ASN.1 parsing code in libUbikeyJni.so, especially Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum and related child/length computation routines, adding strict bounds and length checks on all ASN.1 fields
Add robust input validation at the JNI boundary for jGetCertInfo (e.g., maximum certificate size, structural pre-checks) before passing data into native parsing routines
Run targeted fuzzing with ASan/UBSan enabled against CERT_Load/Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT/Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum to pinpoint the exact memory safety issue and patch it
assumptions: Certificate bytes passed into jGetCertInfo originate from potentially untrusted sources in real application flows
Process termination is due to a memory safety or invalid-access condition inside Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum rather than an intentional, safe abort on clearly invalid inputs
limitations: Could not decompile libUbikeyJni.so or inspect Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum implementation due to tool/server errors
Exact nature of the crash (out-of-bounds read vs write vs assertion) and exploitability beyond DoS is unknown

chain_of_thought: Top native frame below libc is Decode_DER_ASN1_ST inside libUbikeyJni.so; crash is in __memcpy, strongly suggesting an out-of-bounds copy during ASN.1/DER parsing.

The call chain Decode_DER_ASN1_ST -> Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT -> CERT_Load -> Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetCertInfo shows a classic certificate-decoding pipeline implemented in app-native code, not in the harness.

Even without full decompilation, the function names indicate manual ASN.1 / X.509 parsing and likely handling of length fields from DER data, which is a common source of memcpy-based overflows if lengths are not validated against buffer bounds.

The JNI method jGetCertInfo is the bridge; in a real app this would accept certificate bytes from Java (e.g., from a smartcard, NFC, or network), making the DER input attacker-controlled or at least externally controlled. The fuzz harness simply exercises this same entry point with arbitrary inputs, so the path is realistic.

The crash occurs in __memcpy rather than at a controlled abort/sanitizer check, implying that size or pointers passed to memcpy were invalid. That in turn implies missing or insufficient bounds checking in Decode_DER_ASN1_ST or its callers when interpreting DER length fields.

There is no evidence this is a harness-only misuse such as passing impossible JNI arguments (e.g., NULL env) or violating ownership; instead it is feeding arbitrary bytes into legitimate parsing code, which is in line with realistic attacker capabilities.

Because DER parsing is entirely driven by the untrusted byte sequence and the library appears to implement its own ASN.1 decoder, it is highly plausible that the memcpy length is derived from untrusted length fields without robust validation. This is a classic memory-corruption vulnerability (out-of-bounds write or read) with potential for serious exploitation (heap corruption, RCE).

Exact source/destination/length expressions at the crash site are not available here, so there remains some uncertainty, but the combination of function names, memcpy crash, and fuzzing context make a real vulnerability significantly more likely than a benign bug.
reasons: Crash in __memcpy called by Decode_DER_ASN1_ST strongly indicates an out-of-bounds memory operation during ASN.1/DER parsing.
DER/X.509 parsing is entirely driven by external/certificate bytes, which are realistically attacker-controlled via the JNI jGetCertInfo interface.
The failing code resides in app-native parsing logic (libUbikeyJni.so), not the harness, and there is no sign of deliberate aborts or unrealistic JNI misuse.
recommendations: Review and harden Decode_DER_ASN1_ST and related ASN.1/X.509 parsing routines to enforce strict bounds checks before any memcpy/memmove calls, especially when using DER length fields.
Add centralized length and pointer validation utilities for ASN.1 parsing, and use safe bounded-copy routines that clamp sizes to buffer capacities.
Instrument this code with ASan/UBSan and re-run fuzzing with corpus minimization to obtain crashing inputs and pinpoint the exact unsafe copy sites for patching.
assumptions: The DER/X.509 input processed by CERT_Load/Decode_DER_ASN1_ST originates from external or attacker-controllable sources in normal app operation.
The __memcpy crash is due to invalid size or pointers derived from parsed data rather than a corrupted allocator or unrelated harness fault.
limitations: No direct decompiled view of Decode_DER_ASN1_ST or the exact memcpy arguments was available for inspection.
Java-side construction of arguments to jGetCertInfo was not inspected, so exact validation logic, if any, is unknown.

chain_of_thought: Tools are unavailable (404) so I cannot decompile, but I must still assess from the provided stack and context.
Crash is in __memcpy called from Decode_DER_ASN1_ST -> CERT_Load -> Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetCertInfo. This is app-owned libUbikeyJni.so, not a harness-only library.

Fuzzer entry fuzz_one_input likely feeds arbitrary data into the Java bridge, which then passes it natively as certificate/DER bytes. Decode_DER_ASN1_ST suggests custom ASN.1 DER certificate parsing and decoding, which traditionally involves length fields and nested structures.

A memcpy crash typically indicates out-of-bounds read/write, often due to trusting attacker-controlled length fields inside encoded input (here, DER). Since fuzzing found a crash, it's likely that Decode_DER_ASN1_ST or CERT_Load is missing proper bounds checking before memcpy with lengths derived from DER lengths.

The path is realistic: in production, jGetCertInfo will be called on certificate blobs from external smartcard/USB key or network. Those are attacker-controlled or at least untrusted inputs. If malformed, same parser will be exercised. There is no indication of harness-only misuse like forced NULL pointers; the parser function names and memcpy strongly point to a typical parsing bug.
Without code, I cannot pinpoint exact argument taint, but strong evidence: parser function name, memcpy crash, fuzz harness triggering via JNI with blob input, no intermediate validation frame names.

Thus, I classify as a likely genuine memory safety vulnerability (buffer overflow / out-of-bounds) in libUbikeyJni.so, in or near Decode_DER_ASN1_ST/CERT_Load, with medium-high confidence. Severity is high because memcpy overflow can give code execution or at least native process compromise, but we don't see proof of RCE yet.
Limitations: No direct view of arguments to memcpy, no Java call graph details, so precise CWE and exploitability degree are inferred, not fully confirmed.
reasons: Crash occurs in __memcpy within custom DER/ASN.1 certificate parsing code (Decode_DER_ASN1_ST), indicating likely out-of-bounds read/write driven by parsed lengths.

JNI bridge Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetCertInfo operates on certificate data that in real usage originates from external/untrusted sources (smartcard/certificate blobs), so attacker control is realistic.
Fuzzing reached the crash through the real JNI entry and app library (libUbikeyJni.so), not a synthetic harness-only stub, so the issue is within production code paths, not just harness misuse.
recommendations: Review and harden Decode_DER_ASN1_ST and CERT_Load to validate all DER/ASN.1 length fields and buffer sizes before memcpy or similar operations.
Add bounds checks and centralized helpers for safe parsing of nested ASN.1 structures, using checked arithmetic for lengths and offsets.
Re-run fuzzing with ASan/UBSan on an instrumented build of libUbikeyJni.so to localize the exact memcpy call and confirm/patch the overflow condition.

The crashing __memcpy is invoked with length or pointers influenced by parsed DER fields rather than fixed-size internal buffers.
